title: Botany
text:

Research topic 1: Ecology and evolution of ferns
Research topic 2: Development of software for data science

Reproducible data analysis, Thursdays 3rd period

https://www.joelnitta.com

title: Botany
text:
Instructor: Joel Nitta



Born and raised in California
Fourth generation Japanese-American
First came to Japan as high school exchange student

title: Botany
text:
About “Botany”

This course previously had a much broader focus: biodiversity in general
I am an expert on fern evolution, so I decided to change the content this year to botany (the science of plants)
I hope you will find the new theme interesting and continue to take this course, but if you decide otherwise, that is OK

title: Botany
text:
Why plants? Why botany?

Discuss with your neighbor why plants matter

title: Botany
text:
Why plants?



Food to eat
Fiber for clothing
Building materials for houses
Paper for books
Drugs for medicines
Oxygen to breath
Basis of the food chain
Mental well-being

title: Botany
text:
Plant Awareness Disparity

(sometimes called “plant blindness”, but this term is not preferred because it is ableist)
The condition of not being aware of the plants around you, despite their importance
This has real-life consequences!

Conservation bias: more money goes to conserving animals
Education bias: biology focuses mainly on animals

title: Botany
text:
Why botany?



Botany helps us organize plant diversity and understand how plants evolved (and are evolving)

title: Botany
text:
Goals of this course

To cure you of plant awareness disparity!

By the end of the course, you should be able to:

Understand the basic plant body plan and composition of a plant cell
Explain the defining characteristics of the major lineages of land plants and how they are related to each other
Explain how plants are distributed on earth to form ecosystems and biomes

title: Botany
text:
Language

The class is conducted in English.
If needed, you may ask questions in Japanese and I will explain in Japanese.
However, you need to be able to communicate in English and understand the lecture materials and textbook content as provided in English.
If the English level of the class is too high, consider taking a different course.

title: Botany
text:
AI Policy

AI is allowed on a limited basis to assist you with the coursework, not to do it for you
For example, after writing the first draft of the weekly assignment on your own, you may use AI to help correct your English grammar and spelling
Usage of AI to generate the main content of any assignment is prohibited and will receive zero points
Bottom line: you are spending time and money to learn. Letting AI do everything is cheating yourself.

title: Botany
text:
Textbook


“Let’s Botanize” by Ben Goulet-Scotta and Jacob S. Suissa.
Digital version available on https://amazon.co.jp for approx. 1,700 JPY.
The textbook is required for all students.

title: Botany
text:
Textbook



This is not a traditional textbook.
The goal of the book is to help you observe plants
Each chapter starts with a prompt
The reader goes outside and observes plants that fit the prompt

title: Botany
text:
Weekly assignment

Starting from week 2, you will answer two prompts from the book each week by observing plants outside of class
When you make an observation, take a picture of the plant and post it on iNaturalist (more on that soon)
You must submit the observation notes (including explanation, photos, and iNaturalist links) by the time class starts (12:50 PM)
During class, you will choose one of your answers to discuss with the other students

title: Botany
text:
On-demand lecture

There is one on-demand lecture (topic: Phylogenetic Analysis)
The on-demand lecture will be posted soon
The content of the on-demand lecture will appear on the midterm test, so be sure to watch it before the test

title: Botany
text:
Midterm Test

There will be an in-class midterm test on 2026-07-07

Content includes Weeks 2, 3, and 4 plus the on-demand lecture

The test will be administered online (Moodle)

Be sure to bring a laptop computer to take the test

You may use one page of hand-written notes during the test (A4, both sides). No other materials are permitted during the test.

title: Botany
text:
Grading

6 weekly observation reports (50 points each): 50%

40 points for report + 10 points for in-class discussion

Midterm test: 50%

title: Botany
text:
Absence policy

No late assignments will be accepted, and no points will be given for discussion in case of absence.
Make-up assignments will only be given in cases of documented medical reasons or family emergencies.

title: Botany
text:
About iNaturalist



App for citizen science
Allows anybody to easily document the species around them, even if they are not specialists
“Real-life Pokémon”

title: Botany
text:
Installation



Apple


Google

title: Botany
text:
Setting up your account

Feel free to choose any username you like (it does not have to be your real name)
Set all of the data sharing options to “on”

title: Botany
text:
Identifying Species with iNaturalist



Open iNaturalist
“Me”: A list of your observations so far
“+”: Add a new observation

title: Botany
section: Make sure to upload your observation
text:
Make sure to upload your observation



Tap “Upload Now” after making your observation

title: Botany
section: Make sure to upload your observation
text:
Make sure to upload your observation



If your observation has an up arrow in a dotted circle, tap it to upload

title: Botany
text:
Getting the observation URL



Log in to https://www.inaturalist.org/
Click on “Observations”
Click on one of your recent observations to get its URL

title: Botany
text:
Next week’s assignment: Plant Morphology

Prompts

5: Can you find the growing tip of a root?
9: Compare a flower with fused petals to one with separate petals.
16: Can you find the patterns of nodes repeated along a branch?
28: Look for outgrowths where a young leaf attaches to a stem.
29: Can you find a scar on a branch where a leaf used to be attached?

title: Botany
section: All organisms are related by descent from a common ancestor
text:
All organisms are related by descent from a common ancestor

Earth began about 4.5 billion years ago
Life on earth began about 3.6 billion years ago
All life on earth is organized into cells and uses DNA

This indicates a single origin of life on earth
All organisms that live today are related to each other

title: Botany
section: A phylogeny represents how organisms are related
text:
A phylogeny represents how organisms are related

The name of the organism (usually, the species) is written on one side (tips)
The lines connecting the names indicate evolution within that species
Nodes (where lines come together) indicate a common ancestor shared between species
In theory, we can draw the entire evolutionary history of earth using a phylogeny

title: Botany
section: The most important part of a phylogeny is how the lines connect
text:
The most important part of a phylogeny is how the lines connect

The order of tips does not matter (one living species on the phylogeny is not “more evolved” than another)
This may be confusing if you are used to thinking of humans as being “highly evolved”
Since there is one common origin of life on earth, that means all lineages living now are equally old

We just took different pathways to get here

title: Botany
section: We must use indirect evidence to infer how evolution happened
text:
We must use indirect evidence to infer how evolution happened

In most cases, humans cannot watch lineages splitting
We have to use indirect evidence

Inherited characteristics, such as physical appearance or DNA
These are called traits

title: Botany
section: Traits that were inherited from a common ancestor are evidence for phylogeny
text: Traits that were inherited from a common ancestor are evidence for phylogeny

title: Botany
text:
Activity: Build Your Own Phylogeny (BYOP)

In this activity, you will build a phylogeny of the following species:

Fish
Owl
Frog
Starfish
Cow

title: Botany
text:
Classification systems

Taxonomy is the study of naming organisms
A taxonomic classification is a way to organize names
A classification is a hierarchy of nested groups

Each level of the hierarchy is called a taxonomic rank
Each named group is called a taxon (plural: “taxa”)

title: Botany
text:
Typical taxonomic classification

Ranks: kingdom, phylum, order, class, family, genus, species, variety
Taxa:

title: Botany
text:
Classification should reflect evolution

Groups of organisms that we recognize in the classification should share common evolutionary history
Ideally
